summary: This 2006 retrospective examines the state of agile software development five years after the Agile Manifesto was signed. The piece surveys Agile Alliance board members to identify key trends including growing awareness, challenges with scaling and leadership, and concerns about commodification and dilution of agile principles. While agile methods have spread widely across different types of enterprises with good results, the article notes that failures typically occur when teams aren't genuinely applying agile principles and practices. The survey identifies emerging challenges as agile moves from early adoption to mainstream acceptance.
key_points: Agile has 'crossed the chasm' from early adopters to mainstream awareness in the software industry
Four key trends identified: Awareness & Propagation, Leadership & Scaling, Commodification & Dilution, and Emerging Challenges
Agile approaches are being applied successfully across essentially every kind of enterprise
Failures with agile typically stem from not actually applying the principles and practices authentically
The transition from 'Lightweight Methods' to 'Agile' branding in 2001 launched a movement of conferences, books, and organizational support
money_quotes: Seventeen free-thinking software engineers got together to uncover commonalities in their thinking about software development methods.
It is certainly possible to fail using Agile, but the situations where this happens seem to begin by not doing anything that would be recognized as truly applying the principles and practices.
By now, most people in the software industry have heard about Agile software development, whether or not they are clear about what it means.
Agile approaches are being applied effectively in essentially every kind of enterprise, and with good results.
key_insight: Agile failures typically result not from flaws in the methodology itself, but from organizations failing to genuinely apply its core principles and practices.
